title: Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita, 2020
The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) and GDP per capita for countries in 2020, with the horizontal axis plotted on a logarithmic scale to accommodate the wide range of economic output. The vertical axis measures the AHDI score from 0 to roughly 0.9, while the horizontal axis tracks GDP per capita in international dollars at 2011 prices, ranging from under $1,000 to over $100,000. Each circle represents a country, with its size corresponding to the total population, and colors distinguish regions including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data points form a distinct upward curve, showing that higher GDP per capita coincides with higher AHDI scores across the global sample. At the lower end of the distribution, countries such as Burundi, Chad, and the Democratic Republic of Congo cluster with GDP per capita below $2,000 and AHDI scores around 0.2. Moving toward the middle of the chart, nations like India, Indonesia, and Peru occupy the $5,000 to $10,000 range with index scores between 0.4 and 0.5. Large populations in China and India appear as prominent circles in this middle-income band. At the upper end, Switzerland, Japan, and the United States show AHDI scores exceeding 0.8, with GDP per capita figures generally above $40,000. Qatar stands apart on the far right with a GDP per capita exceeding $100,000 but an AHDI score lower than the highest-scoring European nations. The visual pattern displays a positive association where increases in economic output align with improvements in the composite development index, though the rate of improvement in the index slows as GDP per capita reaches the highest levels.
